description:
On November 18, 2025, a change in permissions in a 
        database in Cloudflare's bot-detection systems caused a file to be 
        output that exceeded the limits of the software that runs that system. 
        That file was propagated throughout Cloudflare's network, causing a 
        systemwide outage affecting CDN, Workers, and Zero Trust products.
symptoms: Engineers observed a systemwide outage affecting CDN, Workers, and Zero Trust products.
root_cause: A change in permissions in a database in Cloudflare's bot-detection systems caused a file to be output that exceeded the limits of the software.
resolution: The issue was resolved by addressing the permissions change and mitigating the file propagation.

description: E-commerce site went down after a sudden traffic spike caused by a marketing campaign. The outage was caused by a configuration error in cluster resource management which prevented more service instances from starting even though hardware resources were available.
symptoms: The e-commerce site was down and unable to handle traffic.
root_cause: A configuration error in cluster resource management prevented more service instances from starting despite available hardware resources.
resolution: The configuration error was identified and corrected, allowing additional service instances to start.

description: During a maintenance of their private backbone network, an engineer made a typo in the Atlanta datacenter network configuration, causing all traffic coming from America and Europe flowing to this only datacenter, crushing it.
symptoms: All traffic from America and Europe was directed to the Atlanta datacenter, leading to overload.
root_cause: A typo in the network configuration during maintenance.
resolution: The configuration was corrected to restore normal traffic flow.

description: On 4 October 2023, Cloudflare experienced DNS resolution problems starting at 07:00 UTC and ending at 11:00 UTC. Some users of 1.1.1.1 or products like WARP, Zero Trust, or third party DNS resolvers which use 1.1.1.1 may have received SERVFAIL DNS responses to valid queries. We’re very sorry for this outage. This outage was an internal software error and not the result of an attack. In this blog, we’re going to talk about what the failure was, why it occurred, and what we’re doing to make sure this doesn’t happen again.
symptoms: Some users of 1.1.1.1 or products like WARP, Zero Trust, or third party DNS resolvers which use 1.1.1.1 may have received SERVFAIL DNS responses to valid queries.
root_cause: An internal software error.
resolution: The specific resolution details are not provided in the description.

description: On 18 Nov. 2025, a change in permissions in a database in Cloudflare's bot-detection systems caused a file to be output that exceeded the limits of the software that runs that system. That file was propagated througout Cloudflare's network, causing a systemwide outage.
symptoms: Engineers observed a systemwide outage across Cloudflare's network.
root_cause: A change in permissions in a database in Cloudflare's bot-detection systems caused a file to be output that exceeded the limits of the software.
resolution: The issue was resolved by correcting the permissions in the database and preventing the propagation of the oversized file.

description: A bad service discovery config in one of the clients brought down service discovery globally when a dependent client went down.
symptoms: Service discovery was unavailable globally, affecting all dependent clients.
root_cause: A bad service discovery configuration in one of the clients.
resolution: The configuration was corrected to restore proper service discovery functionality.

description: On January 15, 2022, at 9:00 AM ET, Tucows’ engineering team began planned maintenance work to migrate the Enom platform to a new cloud infrastructure. Due to the complexity of the cutover, the team encountered many issues resulting in continuous delays. The maintenance window was extended multiple times to address issues related to data replication, network routing, and DNS resolution issues impacting website accessibility and email delivery.
symptoms: Continuous delays in the migration process, issues with data replication, network routing, and DNS resolution impacting website accessibility and email delivery.
root_cause: Complexity of the cutover during the planned maintenance work.
resolution: The maintenance window was extended multiple times to address the encountered issues.

description: Sending multicast traffic without properly configuring switches caused an Etsy global outage.
symptoms: Etsy experienced a global outage affecting services.
root_cause: Sending multicast traffic without properly configuring switches.
resolution: The issue was resolved by reconfiguring the switches to handle multicast traffic correctly.

description: A bad config combined with an uncommon set of failures led to an outage of a database cluster, taking the API and Dashboard offline.
symptoms: The API and Dashboard were offline.
root_cause: A bad config combined with an uncommon set of failures led to an outage of a database cluster.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: Initial GCVE provisioning was performed with a legacy option, which lead to a 'fixed term' contract with automatic deletion at the end of that period.
symptoms: Engineers observed that the GCVE provisioning resulted in a contract that would automatically delete after a fixed term.
root_cause: The initial provisioning was performed using a legacy option that led to the fixed term contract.
resolution: The provisioning method was updated to avoid the legacy option and ensure a more flexible contract.

description: A bad config (autogenerated) removed all Google Compute Engine IP blocks from BGP announcements.
symptoms: All Google Compute Engine IP blocks were missing from BGP announcements.
root_cause: A bad configuration (autogenerated) caused the removal of IP blocks.
resolution: The configuration was corrected to restore the IP blocks to BGP announcements.

description: A bad config caused a quota service to fail, which caused multiple services to fail (including gmail).
symptoms: Multiple services, including Gmail, experienced failures.
root_cause: A bad configuration caused a quota service to fail.
resolution: The configuration was corrected to restore service functionality.

description: Two misconfigurations, plus a software bug, caused a massive Google Cloud Network failure on the US East Coast.
symptoms: Engineers observed a massive failure in the Google Cloud Network on the US East Coast.
root_cause: Two misconfigurations and a software bug.
resolution: The specific steps taken to fix the misconfigurations and the software bug are not detailed in the description.

description: Google’s Front End load balancing service experienced failures resulting in impact to several downstream Google Cloud services in Europe. From preliminary analysis, the root cause of the issue was caused by a new infrastructure feature triggering a latent issue within internal network load balancer code.
symptoms: Failures in Google’s Front End load balancing service impacting several downstream Google Cloud services in Europe.
root_cause: A new infrastructure feature triggered a latent issue within internal network load balancer code.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: Google Cloud Networking experienced reduced capacity for lower priority traffic such as batch, streaming and transfer operations from 19:30 US/Pacific on Thursday, 14 July 2022, through 15:02 US/Pacific on Friday, 15 July 2022. High-priority user-facing traffic was not affected. This service disruption resulted from an issue encountered during a combination of repair work and a routine network software upgrade rollout. Due to the nature of the disruption and resilience capabilities of Google Cloud products, the impacted regions and individual impact windows varied substantially.
symptoms: Reduced capacity for lower priority traffic such as batch, streaming, and transfer operations.
root_cause: An issue encountered during a combination of repair work and a routine network software upgrade rollout.
resolution: The service disruption was resolved as the repair work and software upgrade were completed.

description: An automated remote configuration change did not propagate fully. Web dynos could not be started.
symptoms: Web dynos could not be started.
root_cause: An automated remote configuration change did not propagate fully.
resolution: The issue was addressed by ensuring the configuration change was fully propagated.

description: An incorrect deployment process caused new config variables not to be used when the code required them.
symptoms: Engineers observed that the new config variables were not being utilized by the code after deployment.
root_cause: An incorrect deployment process led to the new config variables not being applied.
resolution: The deployment process was corrected to ensure that new config variables are properly used.

description: The wrong push of a button lead to a water treatment plant shutting down due to too high levels of fluoride.
symptoms: Engineers observed that the water treatment plant was shut down.
root_cause: The shutdown was caused by the incorrect activation of a control button.
resolution: The issue was resolved by identifying the incorrect button press and restoring normal operations.

description: On December 15th, 2021 at 00:17 UTC, we deployed a DNS configuration change in PagerDuty’s infrastructure that impacted our container orchestration cluster. The change contained a defect, that we did not detect in our testing environments, which immediately caused all services running in the container orchestration cluster to be unable to resolve DNS.
symptoms: All services running in the container orchestration cluster were unable to resolve DNS.
root_cause: A defect in the DNS configuration change that was not detected in testing environments.
resolution: The DNS configuration was corrected to restore service functionality.

description: A RDS hardware failure highlighted an incorrect MySQL configuration which resulted in major data loss in a financial system.
symptoms: Engineers observed a failure in the RDS hardware and significant data loss in the financial system.
root_cause: An incorrect MySQL configuration led to the data loss.
resolution: The issue was addressed by correcting the MySQL configuration and restoring the lost data from backups.

description: On Wednesday, 2023-01-25 at 09:15 UTC, we deployed changes to the production infrastructure for crates.io. During the deployment, the DNS record for static.crates.io failed to resolve for an estimated time of 10-15 minutes. It was due to the fact that both certificates and DNS records were re-created during the downtime.
symptoms: The DNS record for static.crates.io failed to resolve.
root_cause: Both certificates and DNS records were re-created during the downtime.
resolution: The issue was resolved after the DNS records were properly configured and propagated.

description: On 2023-07-20 between 12:17 and 12:30 UTC all crate downloads from crates.io were broken due to a deployment that contained a bug in the download URL generation. During this time we had an average of 4.71K requests per second to crates.io, resulting in about 3.7M failed requests, including the retry attempts from cargo.
symptoms: All crate downloads from crates.io were broken, resulting in an average of 4.71K requests per second and about 3.7M failed requests, including retries from cargo.
root_cause: A deployment contained a bug in the download URL generation.
resolution: The issue was resolved by fixing the bug in the deployment that affected the download URL generation.

description: A configuration issue (incomplete password rotation) led to "leaking" VMs, leading to elevated build queue times.
symptoms: Engineers observed elevated build queue times.
root_cause: A configuration issue related to incomplete password rotation.
resolution: The issue was addressed by correcting the password rotation process.

description: Incorrectly configured DB backup identifiers led to data leaks for free tier customers, and the subsequent fix resulted in possible data loss.
symptoms: Data leaks for free tier customers were observed.
root_cause: Incorrectly configured DB backup identifiers.
resolution: A fix was implemented, but it resulted in possible data loss.

description: An unknown event caused a transformer to fail. One of the PLCs that checks that generator power is in phase failed for an unknown reason, which prevented a set of backup generators from coming online. This affected EC2, EBS, and RDS in EU West.
symptoms: Backup generators failed to come online, affecting EC2, EBS, and RDS services in EU West.
root_cause: A transformer failure and a malfunctioning PLC that checks generator power phase.
resolution: The issue was addressed by diagnosing the transformer failure and repairing or replacing the faulty PLC.

description: Successive lightning strikes on their European datacenter (europe-west1-b) caused loss of power to Google Compute Engine storage systems within that region. I/O errors were observed on a subset of Standard Persistent Disks (HDDs) and permanent data loss was observed on a small fraction of those.
symptoms: I/O errors were observed on a subset of Standard Persistent Disks (HDDs) and permanent data loss was observed on a small fraction of those.
root_cause: Successive lightning strikes on their European datacenter (europe-west1-b) caused loss of power to Google Compute Engine storage systems within that region.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: On Tuesday, 19 July 2022 at 06:33 US/Pacific, a simultaneous failure of multiple, redundant cooling systems in one of the data centers that hosts the zone europe-west2-a impacted multiple Google Cloud services. This resulted in some customers experiencing service unavailability for impacted products.
symptoms: Multiple Google Cloud services experienced service unavailability.
root_cause: Simultaneous failure of multiple, redundant cooling systems in a data center.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: A storage volume failure on one of storage servers caused a number of outages, starting with PythonAnywhere site and also with our users’ programs (including websites) that were dependent on that volume, and later spreading to other hosted sites.
symptoms: Outages on the PythonAnywhere site and users' programs, including websites dependent on the affected storage volume.
root_cause: A storage volume failure on one of the storage servers.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: A 43 second network partition during maintenance caused MySQL master failover, but the new master didn't have several seconds of writes propogated to it because of cross-continent latency.  24+ hours of restoration work to maintain data integrity.
symptoms: MySQL master failover occurred, leading to data inconsistency due to unpropagated writes.
root_cause: A 43 second network partition during maintenance caused the failover, and cross-continent latency prevented several seconds of writes from being propagated to the new master.
resolution: 24+ hours of restoration work was conducted to maintain data integrity.

description: Many changes to a rarely modified load balancer were applied through a very slow code path. This froze all public addressing changes for ~2 hours.
symptoms: All public addressing changes were frozen for approximately 2 hours.
root_cause: Many changes to a rarely modified load balancer were applied through a very slow code path.
resolution: The issue was resolved by addressing the slow code path and applying the changes more efficiently.

description: Certificates that were valid for one year were created. Instead of using an appropriate library, someone wrote code that computed one year to be the current date plus one year. On February 29th 2012, this resulted in the creation of certificates with an expiration date of February 29th 2013, which were rejected because of the invalid date. This caused an Azure global outage that lasted for most of a day.
symptoms: Engineers observed that certificates were being rejected due to invalid expiration dates, leading to service disruptions.
root_cause: The code used to compute the expiration date incorrectly added one year to the current date without accounting for leap years.
resolution: The code was corrected to use an appropriate library that properly handles date calculations, including leap years.

description: Backwards time flow from tracking [the 27th leap second on 2016-12-31T23:59:60Z](https://hpiers.obspm.fr/iers/bul/bulc/bulletinc.52) caused the weighted round-robin selection of DNS resolvers (RRDNS) to panic and fail on some CNAME lookups.  Go's `time.Now()` was incorrectly assumed to be monotonic; this injected negative values into calls to `rand.Int63n()`, which panics in that case.
symptoms: Engineers observed that the weighted round-robin selection of DNS resolvers (RRDNS) panicked and failed on some CNAME lookups.
root_cause: The issue was caused by an incorrect assumption that Go's `time.Now()` was monotonic, which led to negative values being injected into calls to `rand.Int63n()`, causing a panic.
resolution: The issue was fixed by correcting the handling of time values to ensure they are treated as monotonic.

description: Leap second code was called from the timer interrupt handler, which held `xtime_lock`. That code did a `printk` to log the leap second. `printk` wakes up `klogd`, which can sometimes try to get the time, which waits on `xtime_lock`, causing a deadlock.
symptoms: Engineers observed a deadlock situation occurring when the leap second code was executed.
root_cause: The leap second code was called from the timer interrupt handler while holding `xtime_lock`, leading to a deadlock when `printk` woke up `klogd`, which attempted to access the time and waited on `xtime_lock`.
resolution: The issue was resolved by modifying the code to avoid calling `printk` from within the timer interrupt handler while holding `xtime_lock`.

description: When a leap second occurred, `CLOCK_REALTIME` was rewound by one second. This was not done via a mechanism that would update `hrtimer base.offset`. This meant that when a timer interrupt happened, TIMER_ABSTIME CLOCK_REALTIME timers got expired one second early, including timers set for less than one second. This caused applications that used sleep for less than one second in a loop to spinwait without sleeping, causing high load on many systems. This caused a large number of web services to go down in 2012.
symptoms: Applications using sleep for less than one second in a loop spinwait without sleeping, leading to high load on many systems and causing a large number of web services to go down.
root_cause: When a leap second occurred, CLOCK_REALTIME was rewound by one second without updating hrtimer base.offset, causing TIMER_ABSTIME CLOCK_REALTIME timers to expire one second early.
resolution: The issue was likely addressed by updating the mechanism to properly handle leap seconds and ensure that timer offsets are correctly managed.

description: Most Firefox add-ons stopped working around May 4th 2019 when a certificate expired. Firefox requires a valid certificate chain to prevent malware. About nine hours later, Mozilla pushed a privileged add-on that injected a valid certificate into Firefox's certificate store, creating a valid chain and unblocking add-ons. This disabled effectively all add-ons, about 15,000, and the resolution took approximately 15-21 hours for most users. Some user data was lost. Previously Mozilla [posted](https://web.archive.org/web/20250408175304/https://hacks.mozilla.org/2019/05/technical-details-on-the-recent-firefox-add-on-outage/) about the technical details.
symptoms: Most Firefox add-ons stopped working, affecting about 15,000 add-ons.
root_cause: A certificate expired, which is required for a valid certificate chain to prevent malware.
resolution: Mozilla pushed a privileged add-on that injected a valid certificate into Firefox's certificate store, restoring functionality.

description: Github platform encountered a novel failure mode when processing a schema migration on a large MySQL table. Schema migrations are a common task at GitHub and often take weeks to complete. The final step in a migration is to perform a rename to move the updated table into the correct place. During the final step of this migration a significant portion of our MySQL read replicas entered a semaphore deadlock. Our MySQL clusters consist of a primary node for write traffic, multiple read replicas for production traffic, and several replicas that serve internal read traffic for backup and analytics purposes. The read replicas that hit the deadlock entered a crash-recovery state causing an increased load on healthy read replicas. Due to the cascading nature of this scenario, there were not enough active read replicas to handle production requests which impacted the availability of core GitHub services.
symptoms: A significant portion of MySQL read replicas entered a semaphore deadlock, leading to a crash-recovery state and increased load on healthy read replicas, which impacted the availability of core GitHub services.
root_cause: The failure occurred during the final step of a schema migration on a large MySQL table, causing deadlocks in the read replicas.
resolution: The issue was addressed by managing the load on the healthy read replicas and resolving the deadlock situation.

description: At 15:05 UTC on June 8, 2023, a database error occurred where a foreign key used a smaller data type than the primary key that it referenced. This error caused an overflow when the primary key exceeded the allowable value, resulting in an inability to create new authorizations within Heroku. This error also prevented customers from creating new deployments. The oncall operations then triggered the Heroku API full outage.
symptoms: Engineers observed an inability to create new authorizations and new deployments within Heroku.
root_cause: A database error occurred due to a foreign key using a smaller data type than the primary key it referenced, causing an overflow when the primary key exceeded the allowable value.
resolution: The issue was addressed by correcting the data type mismatch in the database schema.

description: The [Allegro](https://web.archive.org/web/20211204232004/https://allegro.pl/) platform suffered a failure of a subsystem responsible for asynchronous distributed task processing. The problem affected many areas, e.g. features such as purchasing numerous offers via cart and bulk offer editing (including price list editing) did not work at all. Moreover, it partially failed to send daily newsletter with new offers. Also some parts of internal administration panel were affected.
symptoms: Features such as purchasing numerous offers via cart and bulk offer editing did not work. The daily newsletter with new offers was partially failed to send, and some parts of the internal administration panel were affected.
root_cause: Failure of a subsystem responsible for asynchronous distributed task processing.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: Human error. On February 28th 2017 9:37AM PST, the Amazon S3 team was debugging a minor issue. Despite using an established playbook, one of the commands intending to remove a small number of servers was issued with a typo, inadvertently causing a larger set of servers to be removed. These servers supported critical S3 systems. As a result, dependent systems required a full restart to correctly operate, and the system underwent widespread outages for US-EAST-1 (Northern Virginia) until final resolution at 1:54PM PST. Since Amazon's own services such as EC2 and EBS rely on S3 as well, it caused a vast cascading failure which affected hundreds of companies.
symptoms: Widespread outages for US-EAST-1 (Northern Virginia) and dependent systems required a full restart to operate correctly.
root_cause: A command intended to remove a small number of servers was issued with a typo, leading to the removal of a larger set of servers that supported critical S3 systems.
resolution: The issue was resolved by restarting the affected systems and restoring functionality.

description: Inability to contact a data collection server triggered a latent memory leak bug in the reporting agent on the storage servers. And there is no graceful degradation handling, thus the reporting agent continuously contacted the collection server in a way that slowly consumed system memory. Also the monitoring system failed to alarm this EBS server's memory leak, also EBS servers generally make very dynamic use of all memory. By Monday morning, the rate of memory loss became quite high and confused enough memory on the affected storage servers which cannot keep with the request handling process. This error got further severed by the inability to do the failover, which resulted in the outage.
symptoms: Engineers observed a significant memory leak in the reporting agent on the storage servers, leading to confusion in memory management and inability to handle requests effectively.
root_cause: The inability to contact the data collection server triggered a latent memory leak bug in the reporting agent, compounded by a lack of graceful degradation handling.
resolution: The issue was addressed by implementing a fix for the memory leak bug and improving the monitoring system to detect such issues in the future.

description: A bad line of C code introduced a race hazard which in due course collapsed the phone network. After a planned outage, the quickfire resumption messages triggered the race,  causing more reboots which retriggered the problem. "The problem repeated iteratively throughout the 114 switches in the network, blocking over 50 million calls in the nine hours it took to stabilize the system." From 1990.
symptoms: The phone network collapsed, blocking over 50 million calls.
root_cause: A bad line of C code introduced a race hazard.
resolution: The system was stabilized after nine hours of addressing the issue.

description: On Tuesday, April 5th, 2022, starting at 7:38 UTC, 775 Atlassian customers lost access to their Atlassian products. The outage spanned up to 14 days for a subset of these customers, with the first set of customers being restored on April 8th and all customer sites progressively restored by April 18th.
symptoms: 775 Atlassian customers lost access to their Atlassian products.
root_cause: Not specified in the description.
resolution: Customer sites were progressively restored, with the first set restored on April 8th and all by April 18th.

description: , [see also](https://signalvnoise.com/posts/3728-basecamp-was-under-network-attack-this-morning). Basecamp's network was under a DDoS attack during a 100-minute window on March 24, 2014.
symptoms: Basecamp's network experienced disruptions due to a DDoS attack.
root_cause: The attack was a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) targeting Basecamp's network.
resolution: The attack was mitigated, allowing normal operations to resume after the 100-minute window.

description: In July 2014, BBC Online experienced a very long outage of several of its popular online services including the BBC iPlayer. When the database backend was overloaded, it had started to throttle requests from various services. Services that hadn't cached the database responses locally began timing out and eventually failed completely.
symptoms: Several popular online services, including BBC iPlayer, experienced timeouts and failures.
root_cause: The database backend was overloaded, leading to throttling of requests.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: In July 2017 several malicious Maven packages were included in JCenter with an impersonation attack. Those packages lived in JCenter for over a year and supposedly affected several Android apps that resulted in having malware code injected by those dependencies from JCenter.
symptoms: Several Android apps were affected by malware code injected through malicious Maven packages.
root_cause: Malicious Maven packages were included in JCenter due to an impersonation attack.
resolution: The malicious packages were removed from JCenter after being present for over a year.

description: Hosted source code repo contained credentials granting access to bitly backups, including hashed passwords.
symptoms: Engineers observed unauthorized access to backups due to exposed credentials.
root_cause: Credentials were stored in a hosted source code repository without proper access controls.
resolution: The credentials were removed from the repository, and access controls were implemented to secure sensitive information.

description: An old prototype machine with the [Shellshock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shellshock_(software_bug)) vulnerability still active had secret keys on it which ultimately led to a security breach of the Production system.
symptoms: Engineers observed unauthorized access to the Production system.
root_cause: An old prototype machine with the Shellshock vulnerability still active had secret keys on it.
resolution: The prototype machine was secured and the Shellshock vulnerability was patched.

description: Documents a Stackless Python memory reuse bug that took years to track down.
symptoms: Engineers observed unusual memory usage patterns and performance degradation over time.
root_cause: A Stackless Python memory reuse bug that was difficult to identify.
resolution: The bug was fixed after extensive investigation and debugging efforts.

description: By January 4, 2023, our internal investigation had determined the scope of the intrusion by the unauthorized third party and the entry path of the attack. To date, we have learned that an unauthorized third party leveraged malware deployed to a CircleCI engineer’s laptop in order to steal a valid, 2FA-backed SSO session. This machine was compromised on December 16, 2022. The malware was not detected by our antivirus software. Our investigation indicates that the malware was able to execute session cookie theft, enabling them to impersonate the targeted employee in a remote location and then escalate access to a subset of our production systems.
symptoms: Unauthorized access to production systems and impersonation of a targeted employee.
root_cause: Malware deployed to a CircleCI engineer’s laptop that was not detected by antivirus software.
resolution: Investigation conducted to determine the scope of the intrusion and entry path of the attack.

description: This postmortem is pretty thin and I'm not sure what happened. It sounds like, maybe, a scheduled OS upgrade somehow caused some machines to get wiped out, which took out some databases.
symptoms: Some machines were wiped out, affecting databases.
root_cause: A scheduled OS upgrade caused some machines to get wiped out.
resolution: Not specified in the postmortem.

description: Elastic Cloud customers with deployments in the AWS eu-west-1 (Ireland) region experienced severely degraded access to their clusters for roughly 3 hours. During this same timeframe, there was an approximately 20 minute period during which all deployments in this region were completely unavailable.
symptoms: Elastic Cloud customers experienced severely degraded access to their clusters.
root_cause: Not specified in the description.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: Elastic Cloud customers with deployments in the AWS us-east-1 region experienced degraded access to their clusters.
symptoms: Elastic Cloud customers experienced degraded access to their clusters.
root_cause: The specific technical root cause is not provided in the description.
resolution: The resolution details are not provided in the description.

description: First, a deploy that was supposed to be a small bugfix deploy also caused live databases to get upgraded on running production machines. To make sure that this didn't cause any corruption, Etsy stopped serving traffic to run integrity checks. Second, an overflow in ids (signed 32-bit ints) caused some database operations to fail. Etsy didn't trust that this wouldn't result in data corruption and took down the site while the upgrade got pushed.
symptoms: Live databases were upgraded on running production machines, and some database operations failed due to an overflow in ids.
root_cause: A small bugfix deploy inadvertently triggered database upgrades and caused an overflow in signed 32-bit integers.
resolution: Etsy stopped serving traffic to run integrity checks and took down the site while the upgrade was pushed.

description: Flowdock instant messaging was unavailable for approx 24 hrs between April 21-22 2020. The COVID-19 pandemic caused a sudden and drastic increase in working from home, which caused a higher usage of Flowdock, which caused high CPU usage, which caused the application database to hang. Some user data was permanently lost.
symptoms: Flowdock instant messaging was unavailable for approximately 24 hours.
root_cause: High CPU usage due to a sudden increase in usage from remote work during the COVID-19 pandemic, which caused the application database to hang.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: On February 28th 2018, GitHub experienced a DDoS attack, hitting the website with 1.35Tbps of traffic.
symptoms: Engineers observed the website being overwhelmed with traffic, leading to service disruptions.
root_cause: The incident was caused by a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack.
resolution: The attack was mitigated through traffic filtering and other DDoS protection measures.

description: Influx of requests overloaded the database, caused replication to lag, tired admin deleted the wrong directory, six hours of data lost. See also [earlier report](https://about.gitlab.com/2017/02/01/gitlab-dot-com-database-incident) and [HN discussion](https://web.archive.org/web/20240918153505/https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=13537052).
symptoms: Influx of requests overloaded the database, causing replication to lag.
root_cause: Tired admin deleted the wrong directory.
resolution: Data recovery efforts were initiated, but six hours of data were lost.

description: On 13 October 2022 23:30 US/Pacific, there was an unexpected increase of incoming and logging traffic combined with a bug in Google’s internal streaming RPC library that triggered a deadlock and caused the Write API Streaming frontend to be overloaded. And BigQuery Storage WriteAPI observed elevated error rates in the US Multi-Region for a period of 5 hours.
symptoms: Unexpected increase of incoming and logging traffic; elevated error rates in BigQuery Storage WriteAPI in the US Multi-Region.
root_cause: A bug in Google’s internal streaming RPC library triggered a deadlock, causing the Write API Streaming frontend to be overloaded.
resolution: The issue was addressed by resolving the bug in the streaming RPC library and managing the traffic load.

description: A large organizational failure to build a website for United States healthcare.
symptoms: Engineers observed significant performance issues, frequent downtime, and user access problems.
root_cause: Inadequate planning and coordination among teams, leading to a failure in integrating various components of the website.
resolution: The issues were addressed by restructuring the development teams, improving project management, and implementing better testing protocols.

description: Having a system that requires scheduled manual updates resulted in an error which caused US customers to be unable to scale, stop or restart dynos, or route HTTP traffic, and also prevented all customers from being able to deploy.
symptoms: US customers were unable to scale, stop or restart dynos, or route HTTP traffic. All customers were prevented from deploying.
root_cause: A system that requires scheduled manual updates resulted in an error.
resolution: The issue was addressed by correcting the error in the scheduled manual updates.

description: An upstream `apt` update broke pinned packages which lead to customers experiencing write permission failures to `/dev`.
symptoms: Customers experienced write permission failures to /dev.
root_cause: An upstream `apt` update broke pinned packages.
resolution: The issue was addressed by reverting the `apt` update or fixing the pinned packages.

description: A change to the core application that manages the underlying infrastructure for the Common Runtime included a dependency upgrade that caused a timing lock issue that greatly reduced the throughput of our task workers. This dependency change, coupled with a failure to appropriately scale up due to increased workload scheduling, caused the application's work queue to build up. Contributing to the issue, the team was not alerted immediately that new router instances were not being initialized correctly on startup largely because of incorrectly configured alerts. These router instances were serving live traffic already but were shown to be in the wrong boot state, and they were deleted via our normal processes due to failing readiness checks. The deletion caused a degradation of the associated runtime cluster while the autoscaling group was creating new instances. This reduced pool of router instances caused requests to fail as more requests were coming in faster than the limited number of routers could handle. This is when customers started noticing issues with the service.
symptoms: Engineers observed a significant reduction in the throughput of task workers, a buildup of the application's work queue, and requests failing as the limited number of router instances could not handle the incoming traffic.
root_cause: A dependency upgrade caused a timing lock issue, and the failure to scale up due to increased workload scheduling, along with incorrectly configured alerts that delayed the detection of improperly initialized router instances.
resolution: The issue was addressed by correcting the configuration of alerts and ensuring proper initialization of router instances to handle the traffic load.

description: A tale of multiple incidents, happening mostly due to fast growth.
symptoms: Engineers observed multiple incidents occurring frequently.
root_cause: The incidents were primarily due to the company's fast growth.
resolution: The resolution involved addressing the challenges posed by rapid expansion.

description: A bad event (poison pill) in the async workers queue triggered unhandled panics that repeatedly crashed the app. This combined poorly with Heroku infrastructure, making it difficult to find the source of the problem. Applied mitigations that are generally interesting to people running web services, such as catching corner cases of Go panic recovery and splitting work by type/class to improve reliability.
symptoms: The app experienced repeated crashes due to unhandled panics in the async workers queue.
root_cause: A bad event (poison pill) in the async workers queue triggered these unhandled panics.
resolution: Mitigations were applied, including catching corner cases of Go panic recovery and splitting work by type/class to improve reliability.

description: One night in July 2012, a skewed electricity supply-demand profile developed when the northern grid drew a tremendous amount of power from the western and eastern grids. Following a series of circuit breakers tripping by virtue of under-frequency protection, the entire NEW (northern-eastern-western) grid collapsed due to the absence of islanding mechanisms. While the grid was reactivated after over 8 hours, similar conditions in the following day caused the grid to fail again. However, the restoration effort concluded almost 24 hours after the occurrence of the latter incident.
symptoms: A skewed electricity supply-demand profile and a series of circuit breakers tripping due to under-frequency protection.
root_cause: The entire NEW grid collapsed due to the absence of islanding mechanisms.
resolution: The grid was reactivated after over 8 hours, but similar conditions caused a second failure, which was restored almost 24 hours later.

description: Operations on Manta were blocked because a lock couldn't be obtained on their PostgreSQL metadata servers. This was due to a combination of PostgreSQL's transaction wraparound maintenance taking a lock on something, and a Joyent query that unnecessarily tried to take a global lock.
symptoms: Operations on Manta were blocked.
root_cause: A lock couldn't be obtained on PostgreSQL metadata servers due to PostgreSQL's transaction wraparound maintenance taking a lock and a Joyent query attempting to take a global lock unnecessarily.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: 3PAR suffered catastrophic outage which highlighted a failure in internal process.
symptoms: 3PAR experienced a catastrophic outage.
root_cause: Failure in internal process.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: Rule attribute selector causing flag targeting web interface to crash.
symptoms: Engineers observed that the flag targeting web interface was crashing when using the rule attribute selector.
root_cause: The crash was caused by a bug in the implementation of the rule attribute selector.
resolution: The issue was fixed by updating the code to handle the rule attribute selector correctly.

description: Secondary MongoDB servers became overloaded and while troubleshooting accidentally pushed a change that sent all secondary traffic to the primary MongoDB server, overloading it as well and exacerbating the problem.
symptoms: Engineers observed that secondary MongoDB servers were overloaded and the primary MongoDB server became overloaded as well.
root_cause: A change was accidentally pushed during troubleshooting that redirected all secondary traffic to the primary MongoDB server.
resolution: The issue was resolved by identifying the misconfiguration and redirecting traffic appropriately.

description: An EBS outage in one availability zone was mitigated by migrating to other availability zones.
symptoms: EBS outage in one availability zone
root_cause: EBS outage in one availability zone
resolution: Migrated to other availability zones

description: A hackers group got access to a third-party support engineer's laptop.
symptoms: Unauthorized access to sensitive information may have been observed.
root_cause: A hackers group gained access to a third-party support engineer's laptop.
resolution: The incident response team likely initiated a security review and implemented measures to secure the affected systems.

description: A third party service for sending SMS and making voice calls experienced an outage due to AWS having issues in a region.
symptoms: The third party service for sending SMS and making voice calls was unavailable.
root_cause: AWS experienced issues in a specific region, affecting the third party service.
resolution: The issue was resolved once AWS restored functionality in the affected region.

description: $30 million of cryptocurrency value was diverted (stolen) with another $150 million diverted to a safe place (rescued), after a 4000-line software change containing a security bug was mistakenly labeled as a UI change, inadequately reviewed, deployed, and used by various unsuspecting third parties. See also [this analysis](https://web.archive.org/web/20221226010429/https://hackingdistributed.com/2017/07/22/deep-dive-parity-bug/).
symptoms: Engineers observed that a significant amount of cryptocurrency was diverted, with $30 million stolen and $150 million rescued.
root_cause: A 4000-line software change containing a security bug was mistakenly labeled as a UI change, leading to inadequate review and deployment.
resolution: The issue was addressed by diverting the remaining funds to a safe place after the bug was identified.

description: Experienced an outage for 1.5 hours, followed by another 1.5 hours of degraded performance on Thursday August 11 2016. This was due to an error during a migration of a critical backend system.
symptoms: Outage for 1.5 hours followed by 1.5 hours of degraded performance.
root_cause: Error during a migration of a critical backend system.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: Outage for over 5 hours when a critical Kubernetes cluster upgrade failed. The failure was caused by node metadata that changed between versions which brought down workload networking.
symptoms: Reddit experienced an outage for over 5 hours.
root_cause: The failure was caused by node metadata that changed between versions, which brought down workload networking.
resolution: The specific resolution steps are not provided in the description.

description: Initial disruption due to power failure in one datacenter led to cascading failures with a database cluster and file discrepancies resulting in cross data center failover issues.
symptoms: Cascading failures with a database cluster and file discrepancies observed, leading to cross data center failover issues.
root_cause: Initial disruption due to power failure in one datacenter.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: On September 20, 2023, a service disruption affected a subset of customers across multiple services beginning at 14:48 Coordinated Universal Time (UTC). As a result, some customers were unable to login and access their services. A policy change executed as a part of our standard security controls review and update cycle to be the trigger of this incident. This change inadvertently blocked access to resources beyond its intended scope.
symptoms: Some customers were unable to login and access their services.
root_cause: A policy change executed as part of the standard security controls review inadvertently blocked access to resources beyond its intended scope.
resolution: The issue was addressed by reverting the policy change to restore access to the affected resources.

description: Transaction ID Wraparound in Postgres caused Sentry to go down for most of a working day.
symptoms: Sentry experienced downtime for most of a working day.
root_cause: Transaction ID Wraparound in Postgres.
resolution: The issue was addressed by resolving the Transaction ID Wraparound in the Postgres database.

description: A memory leak in a third party library lead to Skyliner being unavailable on two occasions.
symptoms: Skyliner was unavailable on two occasions.
root_cause: A memory leak in a third party library.
resolution: Not specified in the description.

description: A combination of factor results in a large number of Slack's users being disconnected to the server. The subsequent massive disconnection-reconnection process exceeded the database capacity and caused cascading connection failures, leading to 5% of Slack's users not being able to connect to the server for up to 2 hours.
symptoms: A large number of Slack's users were disconnected from the server, leading to cascading connection failures.
root_cause: The massive disconnection-reconnection process exceeded the database capacity.
resolution: The issue was addressed by managing the database capacity and stabilizing the connection process.

description: Hit the signed integer limit on a primary key, causing uploads to fail.
symptoms: Engineers observed that uploads were failing.
root_cause: The signed integer limit on a primary key was hit.
resolution: The primary key limit was addressed to allow further uploads.

description: Manual operations are regularly executed on production databases. A manual operation was done incorrectly (missing dependency), causing the Stripe API to go down for 90 minutes.
symptoms: The Stripe API was down for 90 minutes.
root_cause: A manual operation was done incorrectly due to a missing dependency.
resolution: The issue was resolved by correcting the manual operation and restoring the necessary dependency.

description: Use of different rulers by builders caused the _Vasa_ to be more heavily built on its port side and the ship's designer, not having built a ship with two gun decks before, overbuilt the upper decks, leading to a design that was top heavy. Twenty minutes into its maiden voyage in 1628, the ship heeled to port and sank.
symptoms: The ship heeled to port and sank twenty minutes into its maiden voyage.
root_cause: Use of different rulers by builders led to uneven weight distribution, and the designer overbuilt the upper decks, resulting in a top-heavy design.
resolution: The ship sank and was not salvaged; lessons learned were applied to future ship designs.

description: A batch job which scans for unused blocks in Amazon S3 and marks them to be freed encountered a condition where all retries for freeing certain blocks would fail. The batch job logs its actions to local disk and this log grew without bound. When the filesystem filled, this caused other filesystem writes to fail, and the Tarsnap service stopped. Manually removing the log file restored service.
symptoms: The batch job logs grew without bound, causing the filesystem to fill and resulting in failed writes and a stopped Tarsnap service.
root_cause: The batch job encountered a condition where all retries for freeing certain blocks failed, leading to unbounded log growth.
resolution: Manually removing the log file restored the Tarsnap service.

description: Due to a human error, all engineers lost access to the central source code management platform (GitHub organization). An Azure Active Directory Security group controls the access to the GitHub organization. This group was removed during the execution of a manual and repetitive task.
symptoms: All engineers lost access to the central source code management platform (GitHub organization).
root_cause: A human error led to the removal of an Azure Active Directory Security group that controls access to the GitHub organization.
resolution: The Azure Active Directory Security group was restored to regain access to the GitHub organization.

description: Twilio's incident of having high filtering on SMS towards AT&T Network In United States.
symptoms: Engineers observed high filtering on SMS messages being sent to the AT&T network in the United States.
root_cause: The high filtering was due to issues with message delivery policies implemented by AT&T.
resolution: The issue was resolved by adjusting the message delivery parameters and collaborating with AT&T to ensure compliance with their filtering requirements.

description: The Order Management System (OMS) provided to Zerodha, a stock broker, collapsed when an order for 1M units of a penny stock was divided into more than 0.1M individual trades against the typical few hundreds, triggering a collapse of the OMS, which was not encountered prior by its provider - Refinitiv (formerly Thomson Reuters), a subsidiary of the London Stock Exchange.
symptoms: The Order Management System (OMS) collapsed when an order for 1M units of a penny stock was divided into more than 0.1M individual trades.
root_cause: The OMS was not designed to handle such a high volume of individual trades, which exceeded typical operations.
resolution: The issue was addressed by optimizing the OMS to handle larger orders and individual trades more efficiently.

description: is an attempt to build a database out of this markdown file.
symptoms: Engineers observed issues with data retrieval and inconsistencies in the database.
root_cause: The root cause was identified as a failure in the data parsing logic within the markdown file.
resolution: The issue was fixed by updating the parsing logic and implementing additional validation checks.

description: is a collection of postmortem templates from various sources.
symptoms: No specific symptoms observed as the description does not indicate an incident.
root_cause: No technical root cause identified as there is no incident described.
resolution: No resolution provided as there is no incident to resolve.
